chunks.title: Course Information
chunks.content: Section 500 (46248) Programming I Spring 2026 - College Station Meeting Times: Meeting Type: LEC Meeting Days: MWF Start Time: 12:40PM End Time: 1:30PM Start Date: 01/12/2026 End Date: 05/05/2026 Meeting Location: ZACH 310 Meeting Times: Meeting Type: LAB Meeting Days: TR Start Time: 12:45PM End Time: 1:35PM Start Date: 01/12/2026 End Date: 05/05/2026 Meeting Location: ZACH 596 Credit Hours: 4. Programming I. (3-2). Credit 4. Basic concepts in using computation to enhance problem solving abilities; understanding how people communicate with computers, and how computing affects society; computational thinking; representation of data; analysis of program behavior; methods for identifying and fixing errors in programs; understanding abilities and limitation of programs; development and execution of programs. This is an introductory course designed for any student interested in using computation to enhance their problem-solving abilities. No prior experience in programming is necessary. Students will use their problem-solving abilities to implement programs in Python. Special Course Designation: None. Assignments will require use of standard programming style conventions and documentation which will be illustrated in lab and lecture examples.

chunks.title: Teaching Assistant
chunks.content: Name: Leonardo Frias Name: Kloie Kim Name: Rasheed Mann

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content: Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to: • Develop Computational Thinking: The learner can apply concepts in computation to enhance problemp-solving abilities across various disciplines. • Implement Python Programs: The learner can design, write, and execute functional programs in the Python programming language. • Understand Data Representation: The learner can comprehend how data is internally represented within a computer and how people communicate with computing systems. • Degug and Analyze Behavior: The learner can identify and fix errors while analyzing the behavior and limitations of specific programs.

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content: This material Is: Required Programming in Python 3 ISBN: 978-1-394-02737-8 Authors: Bailey Miller Publisher: zyBooks Notes: How to purchase the textbook: • Sign in or create an account at learn.zybooks.com • Enter zyBooks code: TAMUCSCE110YumSpring2026 • Subscribe (A subscription fee is $69. Students may begin subscribing on December 29, 2025. Subscriptions will last until May 25, 2026.)

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
| Course Grading | |
|---|---|
| 1 Midterm | 30% |
| 9 Programming Assignments | 10% |
| Final exam | 30% |
| 5 Homeworks | 10% |
| 5 Quizzes | 20% | *** No extra credit/extra work will be given at the end of the semester. *** Final grades will be assigned as follows: 90-100 A 80-89 B 70-79 C 60-69 D Below 60 F

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content: Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7). Programming assignments and homeworks should be submitted on or before the due dates. Programming assignment and homework grades will lose 25% for each day after the due dates. Attendance at all lectures is required to ensure that each student understands the informational content of the class and becomes aware of any changes to the tentative class exam, quiz, and assignment schedules as noted later in this document. Absence from a lecture is not a valid excuse to be misinformed about any class activity, and the student maintains the responsibility for all course content.

chunks.content: Spring 2026 CSCE 110 Schedule 1/12 Class begins 1/16 Last day for adding/dropping courses 1/19 Holiday (No class) 1/20 Lab starts 1/23 Homework 1 1/26 Quiz 1 1/30 Homework 1 Due 2/6 Homework 2 2/9 Quiz 2 2/13 Homework 2 Due 2/23 Review 2/25 Midterm 3/6 Homework 3 3/9 - 3/13 Spring Break (No class) 3/16 Quiz 3 3/20 Homework 3 Due 3/27 Homework 4 3/30 Quiz 4 4/3 Reading Day (No class) 4/6 Homework 4 Due 4/10 Homework 5 4/13 Quiz 5 4/14 Q-Drop/Withdrawal Deadline 4/17 Homework 5 Due 4/27 Review, Class ends 5/1 Sections 500-502 Final Exam (10:30 am - 12:30 pm) 5/4 Sections 503-505 Final Exam (3:30 pm - 5:30 pm) List of Topics: • Introduction to Python 1 week • Variables and Expressions 2 weeks • Types, Branches, and Loops 1 week • Functions, Recursion and Strings 3 weeks • Lists, Tuples, Sets, and Dictionaries 2 weeks • Classes 2 weeks • Exceptions 1 week • Modules and Files 1 week • Inheritance 1 week

chunks.title: AI Statement
chunks.content: According to the Texas A&M University Definitions of Academic Misconduct, plagiarism is the appropriation of another person's ideas, processes, results or words without giving appropriate credit (aggiehonor.tamu.edu). You should credit your use of anyone else's words, graphic images, or ideas using standard citation styles. Artificial Intelligence (Al) text generators and natural language processing tools (colloquially, chatbots - such as ChatGPT), audio, computer code, video, and image generators should not be used for any work for this class without explicit permission of the instructor and appropriate attribution. This includes, but is not limited to, i. Creating or revising drafts ii. Editing your work iii. Reviewing a peer's work This excludes pre-existing software additions such as spelling and grammar checkers, which are acceptable.
